retrieved_docs.title: Syndactyly -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: As with any surgical procedure, an infection can occur. Although infection is a relatively rare complication of syndactyly surgery, it has the potential to be devastating if not recognized and addressed promptly with surgical debridement or antibiotics. Recent data suggests a postoperative infection rate of 1.6%. Complications from the surgery are more likely to be present in patients with syndromic presentations and multiple preoperative medical diagnoses. [21] Another potential complication is scar formation and contracture. [5] This can be attributed to split-thickness skin graft use or growth of the surrounding bone. Full-thickness graft use is less associated with contracture and is preferred when possible for this reason. [22] Another common complication is web creep, which is defined as the webspace migrating distally as the patient ages. This can lead to a progressive deformity in the hand. The patient's age during the operation may be an important factor concerning web creep. The patient should be followed at regular intervals after surgery to detect this complication at an early stage. Finally, devascularization of the digit or nerve injury can occur. Only 1 side of the digit must be operated on at a time to ensure that the digit retains a functional digital artery. [2] [3] [4] [5] Devascularization may result in prolonged healing and recovery times or, in an extreme situation, create the potential need for amputation. In the setting of nerve injury, multiple techniques have been described to address digital nerve injury, including primary nerve repair, repair with nerve conduit, and nerve grafting. Digital nerve injury, even after repair, may result in loss of or diminished digital sensation. [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Wound Dehiscence -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Infection - Infected wounds are unable to heal appropriately and become 'stuck' in the inflammation phase. Without proper resolution of wound inflammation, proliferation, and maturation of the wound are unable to occur.

retrieved_docs.title: Vaginal Birth After Cesarean Delivery -- Complications -- Uterine Dehiscence
retrieved_docs.content: Uterine dehiscence is also described. Dehiscence differs from a uterine rupture in that the outer serosal layer of the uterus may remain intact while the underlying muscular layers have opened, allowing for visualization of the amniotic sac and fetus. Such a finding is often referred to as a "uterine window." Patients with uterine dehiscence are frequently asymptomatic, and the more serious sequela that can occur with uterine rupture are less likely to be encountered. When reviewing scholarly articles about uterine scar rupture and dehiscence, the distinction between these entities is not always clear, sometimes making study conclusions difficult to interpret.

retrieved_docs.title: Mucocele and Ranula -- Complications -- Postoperative
retrieved_docs.content: Hematoma Infection Dehiscence of the wound

retrieved_docs.title: Wound Dehiscence -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Radiation - Surgery should be avoided in patients receiving radiation whenever possible. Radiation leads to microvascular obliteration, leading to a gradual decrease in tissue perfusion towards the epicenter of the radiated target area, compromising wound repair. Radiation can also lead to altered cellular replication and increased fibrosis, inhibiting proper wound healing.

retrieved_docs.title: Syndactyly -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: As mentioned in the previous sections, discussing with parents if and when surgery is necessary is important. Surgery is indicated for syndactyly if it is felt the function of the affected extremity can be improved. Educating parents that delaying the surgery until the patient is older improves outcomes unless specific situations, as previously discussed, are involved (border digit involvement and multiple fused digits). Although repeat surgery is relatively uncommon, it is essential to discuss with the family complications such as scar formation and contracture, webspace creep, possible devascularization of the digit, and digital nerve injury.

retrieved_docs.title: Fascial Dehiscence -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The physiology of wound healing involves a well-choreographed pathway. Local tissue factors promote platelet aggregation, acute hemostasis, and chemotaxis. The inflammatory response recruits neutrophils, macrophages, and fibroblasts, which clear cellular debris and begin the formation of a regenerative matrix. Angiogenesis occurs, extracellular matrix components get deposited, and collagen forms and remodels. This process is detailed and complicated, requiring multiple cofactors, enzymatic signals, adequate vascularity, and tissue oxygenation. The process commences immediately upon injury, or in this case, surgical incision and closure, and continues for months with remodeling and maturation. Collagen production begins within 12 to 72 hours of injury and peaks within 7 to 21 days; 80% of tissue strength returns at three months. Due to the complicated nature of wound healing, multiple opportunities for factors detrimental to the process are present.

retrieved_docs.title: Persistent Epithelial Defect -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Infection Ulceration Stromal scarring Melting Perforation

retrieved_docs.title: Wound Dehiscence -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Dehiscence is a partial or total separation of previously approximated wound edges, due to a failure of proper wound healing. This scenario typically occurs 5 to 8 days following surgery when healing is still in the early stages. The causes of dehiscence are similar to the causes of poor wound healing and include ischemia, infection, increased abdominal pressure, diabetes, malnutrition, smoking, and obesity. [1] Superficial dehiscence is when the wound edges begin to separate and by increased bleeding or drainage at the site. The clinician should investigate the wound for worrisome signs, including infection or necrosis. [2] Prompt identification is important for preventing worsening dehiscence, infection, and other complications. Evisceration is a complication of complete wound dehiscence, where intraabdominal organs herniate through the open wound.

retrieved_docs.title: Fascial Dehiscence -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Given the number of open operations performed yearly (approximately 2 million per year in the U.S. from 2009-2013), [5] a notable number of patients have their postoperative course complicated by fascial dehiscence with resultant increases in costs, length of stay, need for additional interventions, and complications.

retrieved_docs.title: Scar Revision -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Complications are typically minimal in scar revision surgery. Early complications include bleeding from the surgical site, pain, reaction to the suture material, surgical site infection, or flap necrosis. Surgical site infections are most commonly due to methicillin-resistant coagulase-positive Staphylococcus (MRSA). [18] Delayed problems include new or recurrent contracture, hypertrophic scar, changes in skin pigmentation, nerve injury, skin atrophy, or trap-door deformity. It is crucial to educate patients on the fact complications could lead to a less desirable scar.

retrieved_docs.title: Cleft Lip -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Hypertrophic scarring Poor cosmesis

retrieved_docs.title: Fascial Dehiscence -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Fascial dehiscence is a concerning complication of open surgical intervention, which often results in the need for additional surgical intervention; dehiscence also represents a significant influence on postoperative morbidity and mortality. High clinical suspicion is essential for early identification and treatment to prevent short- and long-term complications such as chronic wounds, hernias, and evisceration. Optimizing patient factors and surgical techniques can limit the rates of development of fascial dehiscence and thus spare the patient the associated morbidities and need for additional surgical procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: Cleft Lip Repair -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: The complications related to surgical repair are wound dehiscence, scar contracture, scar hypertrophy, and infection. Other complications are related to lip and nasal deformities not resolved during the primary repair, such as vermillion notching, misalignment of the white roll, orbicularis discontinuity, short or excessive length of the lip, short or deviated columella, the horizontal orientation of the nares, abnormalities of nostril size,  and disturbance of alar base position.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 5 -- Community 2925
retrieved_docs.content: The condition described as “CAESAREAN SECTION SCAR TYPE UNKNOWN” is clinically associated with “ولادة قبل الأوان” (preterm birth). This relationship suggests that when a caesarean section scar cannot be classified, there may be an increased likelihood of delivering before term, highlighting the importance of careful obstetric monitoring and management in patients with indeterminate scar types.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 1845
retrieved_docs.content: CAESAREAN SECTION SCAR TYPE UNKNOWN is clinically associated with cesarean section, indicating that patients who have undergone a cesarean delivery may present with a scar of unspecified type, a factor that clinicians should consider when evaluating postoperative healing, potential complications, and future obstetric care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 498
retrieved_docs.content: Abnormality of organs AND/OR soft tissues of pelvis affecting pregnancy encompasses a range of pelvic conditions that can influence gestation, and one specific manifestation of this category is the uterine scar from previous surgery affecting pregnancy. This uterine scar is recognized as a direct consequence of a previous cesarean section complicating pregnancy or childbirth, which itself is classified under the broader parent concept of previous c‑section. The mapping of the cesarean‑related complication to the uterine scar highlights that surgical scarring of the uterus is a key factor linking prior operative deliveries to subsequent pregnancy outcomes. In clinical practice, the presence of a uterine scar is used to interpret pregnancy observable findings, and it is associated with the term إجهاض معتاد (regular miscarriage), indicating that scarred uterine tissue can contribute to spontaneous loss of pregnancy. Together, these relationships underscore the importance of evaluating pelvic organ and soft‑tissue abnormalities, especially uterine scarring from prior surgery, when assessing pregnancy observations and managing patients with a history of cesarean delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1702
retrieved_docs.content: CAESAREAN SECTION SCAR TYPE UNKNOWN and High risk are both clinically associated with cesarean section, indicating that an unidentified type of cesarean scar and a high‑risk patient status are relevant factors in the consideration, planning, and postoperative management of cesarean deliveries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 2 -- Community 1021
retrieved_docs.content: The condition described as “Uterine scar from previous surgery affecting pregnancy” is a specific type of “Abnormality of organs AND/OR soft tissues of pelvis affecting pregnancy.” This uterine scar typically occurs after a surgical procedure in the uterus (“عملية في الرحم”). When clinicians assess a “Pregnancy observable,” the findings are interpreted in the context of a uterine scar, and such interpretation can help explain the presence of “إجهاض معتاد” (habitual or recurrent miscarriage). Consequently, the existence of a uterine scar from prior uterine surgery is an important factor in evaluating observable signs of pregnancy and understanding its potential complications, including recurrent miscarriage.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 141
retrieved_docs.content: as to episode of care or not applicable, is related to an unspecified indication for care or intervention related to labor and delivery, reflecting how undefined obstetric conditions influence clinical management. Pregnancy observable can interpret habitual abortion (إجهاض معتاد) and uterine scar from previous surgery affecting pregnancy, indicating that observable signs may reveal recurrent miscarriage risk and scar‑related sequelae. The uterine scar is mapped to previous cesarean section complicating pregnancy or childbirth, which is a subcategory of previous c‑section. Overall, the knowledge base integrates generic pelvic organ abnormalities, specific uterine and cervical conditions, prior cesarean sections, fetal disorders, anesthesia‑related maternal complications, vascular pelvic lesions, fetal demise, and undefined obstetric indications within a comprehensive framework that guides clinicians in assessing how each factor may affect maternal and fetal outcomes and informs

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1802
retrieved_docs.content: CAESAREAN SECTION SCAR TYPE UNKNOWN is clinically associated with cesarean section, and cesarean section is clinically associated with low transverse cesarean section, indicating that the unknown scar type likely relates to the low transverse incision commonly used in cesarean deliveries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 847
retrieved_docs.content: Abdomen destructive procedure is the broader category that includes the specific operative technique of excision; lysis peritoneal adhesions. This procedure is performed indirectly on the peritoneum, targeting adhesions within the serosal membrane of the abdominal cavity. The peritoneum itself serves as the anatomical site where clinical findings such as فتق البطن (abdominal hernia) are identified, and it is also a location susceptible to اِضطِراب صِفَاقِيّ (membranous or serosal disorder). Recognizing that excision; lysis peritoneal adhesions acts on the peritoneum to resolve adhesions, while the peritoneum may also be involved in hernias and membranous disorders, provides clinicians with a comprehensive view of how therapeutic interventions, diagnostic findings, and pathological conditions are interconnected in abdominal care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 1288
retrieved_docs.content: and the English phrase “maternal care for other type of scar from previous cesarean delivery,” indicating that both the presence of a prior cesarean section and the need for maternal care related to any scar from that surgery are understood under the same underlying clinical entity of a previous cesarean operation. Collectively, these relationships illustrate that pelvic‑organ abnormalities, cervical insufficiency, and uterine scarring—whether described in English, Dutch, or Arabic—are interrelated factors that influence maternal care decisions and are associated with an increased risk of labor complications. Recognizing the hierarchical and cross‑lingual connections among these concepts supports comprehensive assessment and management of pregnant patients with a history of cesarean delivery or pelvic‑organ pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2289
retrieved_docs.content: Abnormality of organs AND/OR soft tissues of pelvis affecting pregnancy is a broad category of conditions that can impact gestation, and within this category the specific condition Uterine scar from previous surgery affecting pregnancy represents a uterine scar that develops after a surgical procedure on the uterus (عملية في الرحم). The uterine scar is therefore classified as a particular pelvic organ/soft‑tissue abnormality that influences pregnancy outcomes. In clinical practice, the presence of such a uterine scar is taken into account when interpreting Pregnancy observable, as it may affect the assessment of fetal well‑being and maternal health. Recognizing that the scar occurs subsequent to عملية في الرحم helps clinicians anticipate potential complications and tailor prenatal monitoring accordingly.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2528
retrieved_docs.content: CAESAREAN SECTION SCAR TYPE UNKNOWN is clinically associated with مخاض قبل الأوان (preterm labor), indicating that an unidentified type of caesarean section scar may be linked to the occurrence of premature labor. In addition, إنهاء الحمل - اختياري (elective termination of pregnancy) is also clinically associated with مخاض قبل الأوان, suggesting that elective pregnancy termination can be related to the development of preterm labor. Together, these associations highlight that both an unknown caesarean section scar type and elective termination of pregnancy are relevant clinical factors to consider when evaluating the risk or presence of preterm labor in patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 777
retrieved_docs.content: and uterine scars from previous surgeries, including previous cesarean sections, are specific indications that belong to the larger group of maternal‑care indications triggered by known or suspected pelvic‑organ abnormalities. These indications guide clinical decision‑making throughout pregnancy, labor, and delivery, and they are consistently represented across Dutch and Arabic terminology, ensuring unified understanding and management of maternal health risks.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 1077
retrieved_docs.content: cesarean section complicating pregnancy or childbirth. These scar‑related and surgical histories feed into the larger category of pelvic organ and soft‑tissue abnormalities that can complicate pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium, with cervical incompetence representing a distinct clinical manifestation within this spectrum and overall contributing to labor complications. Understanding these interrelated classifications aids clinicians in recognizing how past uterine surgery, scar tissue, and specific pelvic abnormalities collectively influence maternal risk and guide appropriate obstetric care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 1 -- Community 75
retrieved_docs.content: Abnormality of organs AND/OR soft tissues of pelvis affecting pregnancy is a broad diagnostic category that includes the more specific condition Uterine scar from previous surgery affecting pregnancy, which is recognized as a type of pelvic‑organ abnormality that can compromise gestation. Within the related category Abnormality of organs and soft tissues of pelvis complicating pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium, specific sub‑entities such as Cervical incompetence complicating pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium are listed as children, and the category is linked to labor complications (مضاعفات المخاض). This abnormality group is also described as having a parent relationship with Previous cesarean section complicating pregnancy or childbirth, indicating that a prior cesarean delivery is a major antecedent that gives rise to these pelvic‑organ complications. Cervixinsufficiëntie als indicatie voor zorg bij moeder (cervical insufficiency as an indication for maternal care) is a

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 1 -- Community 75
retrieved_docs.content: assessing pregnancy outcomes. Finally, Previous cesarean section complicating pregnancy or childbirth is mapped to Uterine scar from previous surgery affecting pregnancy and is shown to have a parent relationship with the generic entity previous c-section, underscoring the hierarchical link from the general prior cesarean event to its specific obstetric complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 1697
retrieved_docs.content: A caesarean section scar of unknown type and a poor reproductive history are clinically associated with مخاض قبل الأوان (preterm labor), indicating that women who have an indeterminate type of previous cesarean scar or who have experienced suboptimal reproductive outcomes are at increased risk for premature labor. Recognizing these associations enables clinicians to identify high‑risk patients, implement closer surveillance, and consider preventive strategies to reduce the likelihood of preterm delivery.
